title: Phrasal verbs: choose the right one
lesson: A phrasal verb is a verb plus a particle (up, off, after…) whose meaning is often idiomatic — 'give up' means quit, 'take after' means resemble. The particle changes the meaning entirely, so they're best learned as whole units rather than guessed from the parts.

items.text: Please {1} your shoes before entering.
items.explanation: 'take off' means to remove an item of clothing.

items.text: She {1} smoking last year and {2} yoga instead.
items.explanation: 'give up' means to quit a habit; 'take up' means to start a new activity or hobby.

items.text: She {1} her grandmother; they have the same eyes.
items.explanation: 'take after' means to resemble an older relative.

items.text: Can you {1} the baby while I'm out?
items.explanation: 'look after' means to take care of someone or something.

items.text: The meeting was {1} until next week, so I {2} the free afternoon to {3} some emails.
items.explanation: 'put off' = postpone; 'used the time to' = made use of it; 'catch up on' = deal with things you are behind on.

items.text: We ran {1} of milk this morning.
items.explanation: 'run out of' means to have no more of something left.

items.text: He {1} the form, {2} the details carefully, and then {3} the document to the office.
items.explanation: 'fill in' = complete a form; 'look over' = review; 'hand in' = submit to someone above you.

items.text: Don't forget to {1} the lights when you leave the room.
items.explanation: 'turn off' means to switch off a light or machine. With a full noun object you can say 'turn off the lights' or 'turn the lights off'.

items.text: I can't make {1} what this sign says; the writing is too faint.
items.explanation: 'make out' means to manage to see, hear or understand something with difficulty.

items.text: The negotiations finally {1} after months of disagreement, and both sides signed the deal.
items.explanation: 'work out' (intransitive here) means to develop in a successful way or end well.

items.text: She {1} the meeting because too many people were ill.
items.explanation: 'call off' means to cancel a planned event. With a full noun you can say 'called off the meeting' or 'called the meeting off'.

items.text: We need to {1} the broken heater before winter arrives.

blanks.hint: fix or deal with a problem

items.explanation: 'sort out' means to resolve or deal with a problem; 'sort out the heater' or 'sort the heater out' are both correct.

items.text: I don't know how she {1} her noisy upstairs neighbours for so many years.
items.explanation: 'put up with' means to tolerate or endure something unpleasant without complaining.

items.text: While clearing out the attic, I {1} a box of old photographs from my childhood.
items.explanation: 'come across' means to find or meet something or someone by chance; 'come down with' = catch an illness, and 'come up against' = face a difficulty, so neither fits here.

items.text: I don't want to {1} the subject of money at dinner; it always starts an argument.
items.explanation: 'bring up' means to mention or introduce a subject in conversation. 'bring about' = cause something to happen, and 'bring round' = revive someone or persuade them, so neither fits a topic of conversation.

items.text: It took him months to {1} the death of his father.
items.explanation: 'get over' means to recover from an illness, shock or upsetting experience. 'get through' = finish or survive a difficult period (and needs the period itself as object), and 'get by' = manage with limited means, so neither expresses recovering from a bereavement.

items.text: The police promised to {1} the complaint and report back within a week.
items.explanation: 'look into' means to investigate or examine a matter. 'look up' = find information in a reference source, and 'look on' = watch something happen without taking part, so neither fits investigating a complaint.

items.text: Our car {1} on the motorway, so we had to wait two hours for a mechanic.
items.explanation: 'break down' means (of a vehicle or machine) to stop working. 'break up' = end a relationship or split into pieces, and 'break off' = stop suddenly or detach a part, so neither describes a car failing mechanically.

items.text: Despite the criticism, the team {1} with the project exactly as they had planned.
items.explanation: 'carry on (with something)' means to continue doing it. 'carry out' = perform or complete a task (and would take the project as a direct object, not 'with'), and 'carry off' = succeed in doing something difficult, so neither fits 'continued as planned'.

items.text: She {1} a bit of Italian while she was living in Rome, just by talking to people.
items.explanation: 'pick up' a language or skill means to learn it informally, without deliberate study. 'pick on' = treat someone unfairly or bully them, and 'pick out' = select or distinguish one thing from others, so neither fits acquiring a language casually.

items.text: The design team need to {1} a fresh concept before tomorrow's pitch to the client.

blanks.hint: think of or devise (an idea, plan)

items.explanation: 'come up with' means to think of or produce an idea or plan. 'come round to' = gradually change your mind and agree to something, and 'come down with' = start to suffer from an illness, so neither fits devising a concept.

items.text: The doctor advised him to {1} sugary drinks and salty snacks if he wanted to lower his blood pressure.
items.explanation: 'cut down on' means to reduce the quantity of something you consume or do. 'cut off' = disconnect, remove or isolate, and 'cut up' = divide into pieces, so neither expresses reducing your intake.

items.text: I can't believe the examiners let him {1} copying half of his answers.

blanks.hint: escape punishment or blame for something

items.explanation: 'get away with' means to do something wrong without being caught or punished. 'get on with' = continue doing something or have a good relationship, and 'get round to' = finally find the time to do something, so neither expresses escaping the consequences of cheating.

items.text: They offered him a promotion in another city, but he {1} because he didn't want to move his family.
items.explanation: 'turn down' means to reject or refuse an offer, application or request. 'turn up' = increase the level of something or arrive/appear, and 'turn over' = flip something or hand it to the authorities, so neither expresses declining the promotion.

items.text: The company has agreed to {1} three new apprentices to cope with the extra orders this autumn.
items.explanation: 'take on' means to employ or hire someone, or to accept a task or responsibility. 'take in' = absorb information or deceive someone, and 'take over' = assume control of something, so neither fits hiring new staff.

items.text: After years of saving, she finally {1} her own catering business last spring.
items.explanation: 'set up' means to establish or start a business, organisation or system. 'set off' = begin a journey (or trigger something), and 'set about' = begin doing a task, so neither fits founding a business.

items.text: You can always {1} me to lend a hand when you move house at the weekend.

blanks.hint: rely on or depend on someone

items.explanation: 'count on' means to rely on or depend on someone or something. 'count in' = include someone in an activity, and 'count out' = exclude someone (or count things one by one), so neither expresses relying on a person.
